reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing and school predictability (child-centred values); equity of access to quality instruction (public-good frame). The stance is conditional on measurable reduction in coaching pressure on age-10 children and verifiable SES-fair access.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children (ages 6–14), especially those without resources for test preparation; families seeking predictable, low-pressure school transitions; interest in geographic stability and commuting feasibility.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system in which access to quality instruction is not mediated by family wealth or test-prep markets; childhood wellbeing protected from high-stakes selection pressure at age 10.
reactions.argument: S1 appeals to our core interest insofar as it promises to reduce the test-preparation arms race that currently pressurizes age-10 children and advantages wealthy families. A less-coachable assessment, if it functions, would reduce the private tutoring industry's leverage over admission decisions. However, the scenario itself acknowledges that the SES indicator does not yet exist, and informal selection (interviews, discretionary criteria) can easily reproduce the skew the reform intends to fix—this risk is material because it means we could end up with the same inequality under a new label, still no predictability, and still high pressure on children. We would support this *only if*: (1) the SES indicator is piloted and validated before nationwide rollout, with explicit thresholds for gaming-resistance; (2) church/foundation schools are brought into binding compliance, not left to opt out; and (3) annual monitoring of admission interviews and discretionary practices is published, so we can see whether informal selection is re-emerging. Without those conditions, the reform risks substituting one opaque selection mechanism for another.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'support' if: (a) a validated, gaming-resistant SES indicator is demonstrated in a 2–3 year pilot before rollout; (b) non-state maintainers are legally bound to the reformed rules with enforcement provisions; (c) independent annual audits of admission interview and discretionary-criteria use are published. We would move to 'oppose' if informal selection practices emerge at scale in pilot districts without remedial action, or if the reform is rolled out without a functioning SES indicator.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing and school predictability; equity of access; geographic stability and commuting. The stance reflects the tension between the reform's stated goal (equity) and its own evidence base, which identifies a near-certain mechanism (non-state offset) that undermines the goal.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children, especially in rural and urban general-school catchments; families committed to public education; families without the resources to switch to non-state schools if general-school quality declines.
reactions.public_good_frame: A public education system that does not abandon lower-SES children to residual, under-resourced schools as middle-class families exit to non-state alternatives; stability and predictability for children whose families cannot afford to navigate a fragmenting school landscape.
reactions.argument: S2 is predicated on caps on state-maintained selective intake, but the scenario's own implementation section explicitly identifies that non-state maintainers will 'expand their own selective intake to absorb demand, replacing rather than eliminating early selection.' This is not an uncertainty—it is flagged as 'well documented' and 'near-certain.' What this means is: shrinking state seats does not reduce selection, it relocates selection to church and foundation schools outside state quota control and harder to regulate for equity. From the perspective of a parent committed to public education or without the means to navigate non-state options, this is not a gain—it is a institutional fragmentation that makes the public system more residual and less predictable. Meanwhile, children remaining in state general schools face both lower peer composition (the high-achievers have left for non-state selective tracks) and potentially lower resource levels as enrollment shrinks without accompanying per-capita funding increases. The scenario acknowledges this residual-skew effect is 'mechanical and near-certain,' yet proceeds anyway. We cannot support a reform whose own evidence base predicts it will fail to achieve its stated goal and will instead make the public system less attractive and more unequal.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'conditional' if: (a) the reform is paired with a binding governance agreement preventing non-state maintainers from expanding selective intake as state caps tighten (enforceable, not merely aspirational); (b) per-capita funding to state general schools is explicitly increased as enrollment shrinks, with legal guarantees insulating education from fiscal consolidation cuts; (c) regular independent monitoring shows that peer composition in general schools is not worsening and that non-state selective enrollment is not growing faster than historical trends. Without these safeguards, the reform amounts to managed decline of public education with selected vulnerability to middle-class exit.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing (delayed selection, longer mixed-peer exposure, reduced age-10 pressure); equity and public-good framing (common comprehensive school to age 14); geographic stability and public-system strength (keeps high-achievers in local schools longer, supporting rural school viability).
reactions.interest: Parents of young children in all socioeconomic strata, especially rural and urban general-school families; families seeking to keep children in local, neighborhood schools; families wanting to avoid early, high-stakes selection pressure; families committed to public comprehensive education.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system in which selection is delayed to age 14–15, children experience longer exposure to mixed-ability peer groups, and public comprehensive schools are not hollowed out by early selective drainage; childhood free from age-10 high-stakes sorting; stronger local school networks that do not collapse into rural deserts as top performers drain to urban selective hubs.
reactions.argument: S3 aligns with our deepest interests: it removes the age-10 selection event that creates pressure on young children, keeps high-achievers in general schools longer (supporting peer composition and rural school viability), and follows a well-evidenced cross-national pattern (Hanushek & Woessmann) that delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome inequality without harming mean performance. Delaying selection is the most direct way to reduce the test-prep arms race and the anxiety it creates around age-10 children. However, the scenario itself identifies a critical risk: middle-class and church/foundation-school flight to inter-school choice and non-state selective alternatives. If comprehensive-school abolition triggers large-scale exit to non-state schools, the remaining public comprehensive system could become more, not less, socioeconomically skewed—the institutional form changes but the sorting mechanism relocates. This is not a hypothetical: the scenario notes the Polish precedent of a pedagogically successful reform (PISA scores rose) that was politically reversed years later under middle-class pressure. We can support S3 *only if* the structural change is accompanied by choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards that are binding and enforced, not merely aspirational. That means: (1) strict regulation of non-state selective admissions or expansion during the transition; (2) transparency in school performance and admissions practices so that choice is not driven by reputation/brand effects amplifying SES sorting; (3) sufficient resources and teacher support in comprehensive schools to make them genuinely attractive to middle-class families; (4) cross-party pacts committing to the reform across electoral cycles. Without those safeguards, we risk trading one inequitable system for another while losing the stability and predictability we need.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'support' if: (a) the reform explicitly links structural abolition to binding choice-architecture and maintainer-oversight reforms, with enforcement provisions and regular monitoring; (b) a national teacher-retraining capacity assessment is completed and resourced before rollout, addressing documented shortages in math and sciences; (c) cross-party legislative commitments are secured covering at least two electoral cycles, with explicit Go/No-Go review points; (d) a pilot in 3–4 districts tracks inter-school choice and non-state enrollment shifts before nationwide rollout, with an explicit threshold for 'sorting migration' at which rollout is suspended. We would move to 'oppose' if: the reform proceeds without those safeguards, or if pilot data shows significant non-state enrollment growth without regulatory response.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing and school predictability; the concern that 'compensation' legitimizes selection as a permanent institutional feature rather than addressing it; equity of access (root cause, not symptom treatment).
reactions.interest: Parents of young children in general-school catchments, especially low-SES families; families seeking to avoid the pressure and inequality of early selection; families dependent on predictable, adequately resourced public schools in their locality.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system that does not mechanically sort children at age 10 and then attempt to compensate for the inequities that sorting produces; childhood free from high-stakes selection; sustainable, predictable public education not dependent on perpetual compensatory programs vulnerable to budget cuts.
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally about managing, not addressing, the early selection problem. It retains the sorting mechanism (6- and 8-year entry at age 10) and then tries to offset the downstream inequities with targeted funding and curriculum standards. The scenario itself flags this: 'compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine, since it removes political pressure for structural reform while leaving selection itself untouched.' This matters profoundly for us as parents: S4 offers no relief from the age-10 selection pressure that our children experience; it offers only a promise that if they do not get into the selective track, they will be compensated later with better resources. But compensation at age 11+ cannot undo the damage already done by being sorted out at age 10, and it cannot restore the peer-composition advantage that the selected children have already accrued. Moreover, S4 explicitly acknowledges that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age (10–12)' — meaning the scenario is built on an assumption it cannot verify. The Portuguese precedent is also not transferable: Portugal did not have Hungary's early tracking, so its TEIP-style funding gains were achieved without that structural inequality in place to begin with. Finally, the scenario notes that 'targeted funding programs are historically the first line item cut during fiscal consolidation'—meaning that after election cycles and budget crises, families in general schools would lose the compensatory resources they depended on, while the selection mechanism remains. We are being asked to accept permanent, structural inequity in exchange for non-structural, politically fragile compensatory spending. That is not a sustainable or credible path to equity for our children.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'conditional' only if: (a) S4 is explicitly paired with a sunset clause: targeted funding is provided for a defined transition period (say, 5 years) after which the system moves either to S1 (reformed admission rules) or S3 (comprehensive to age 14)—compensation is not a permanent substitute for structural change; (b) TEIP-style funding is ring-fenced by constitutional amendment or supermajority legislative requirement, with a fixed percentage of the education budget allocated regardless of fiscal cycles, backed by independent enforcement; (c) a Hungary-specific pilot study is conducted comparing compensated vs. non-compensated general schools on SES-outcome gradients over 5+ years, with an explicit threshold for 'genuine offsetting' below which the reform is terminated in favor of S1 or S3. Without those conditions, we cannot in good conscience support a reform that legitimizes inequality as permanent while depending on politically fragile funding to manage its effects. Our children's wellbeing and predictability cannot rest on annual budget negotiations.
